deficits.missing: In InstructionDataset._format, full_text (which begins with a literal '<s>') is tokenized with default add_special_tokens=True while the prompt-only encoding uses add_special_tokens=False. For Llama-2-style tokenizers this produces a duplicate BOS in input_ids and makes prompt_len off by one, leaving the final prompt token unmasked (it receives loss). This is exactly the 'mask boundary' bug class the chapter itself warns about in 'Assistant prefix contamination'.
deficits.fix: Tokenize full_text with add_special_tokens=False as well (the template already contains '<s>'/'</s>'), or drop the literal '<s>'/'</s>' from the template and let the tokenizer add specials consistently for both encodings; add an assert that input_ids[:prompt_len] equals prompt_enc ids.

deficits.missing: In the rollout() function, resp_mask is set to 1.0 for positions plen..s.shape[0], but model.generate with num_return_sequences pads early-finishing sequences in the group with eos/pad tokens up to the longest completion; those trailing pad tokens are included in the response mask and thus contribute to the policy-gradient loss and KL.
deficits.fix: Mask out tokens after the first EOS in each generated completion (e.g. compute per-sequence generation length via (out[g, plen:] != eos).cumprod or by locating the first eos), or note explicitly that including one EOS is intended and everything after it must be masked.

deficits.missing: The worked numerical example computes the group standard deviation as the population std (divide by G), but the reference code uses torch.Tensor.std(dim=1), which defaults to the Bessel-corrected sample std (divide by G-1). A reader checking the code against the worked numbers will get slightly different advantages (0.5545 vs 0.6403 for the G=4 example).
deficits.fix: Either pass correction=0 (or unbiased=False) in g.std(...) to match the worked example, or add one sentence noting the population/sample-std convention difference and that either is acceptable as long as it is consistent.

deficits.missing: The GRPO chapter's toy driver is commented out and no expected end-to-end training signature is given (e.g. 'mean_reward should climb from ~0.1 to >0.9 within ~100 steps on the toy arithmetic task with Qwen2.5-0.5B-Instruct'). The verifier code in 5.9 has assert-based tests, but the training loop itself has no expected-values check a reader can use to confirm their implementation converges.
deficits.fix: Add a short 'expected behavior' note after the driver: expected mean-reward trajectory on the toy task, roughly how many steps/minutes on a single consumer GPU, and one or two diagnostic thresholds (fraction of non-degenerate groups, entropy) that indicate a healthy vs. broken run.

evidence: 05/01 contains a complete ~300-line runnable SFT loop (sft_train.py) with IGNORE_INDEX=-100 response-only masking, explicit shift ('shift_logits = logits[:, :-1, :]', 'shift_labels = labels[:, 1:]'), padding via collate_fn with labels padded to IGNORE_INDEX, grad accumulation, cosine schedule, clipping, plus a memory budget (full-FT ~110-120GB vs QLoRA ~10-15GB) and a tokens/sec + optimizer-step walkthrough.

05/02 provides a from-scratch ChatML formatter and build_chatml_loss_mask returning (input_ids, loss_mask) with a worked example counting exactly which ~3 of 26 tokens carry loss — multi-turn assistant-only masking is fully specified.

05/08 gives a complete, self-contained GRPO trainer (Qwen2.5-0.5B-Instruct): rollout with num_return_sequences=G, group z-score advantage with eps=1e-4, per-token importance ratio, decoupled clip (0.2/0.28), k3 KL estimator with code, multi-epoch updates with cached old_lp/ref_lp, and concrete hyperparameters (G=8, lr=1e-6, PPO_EPOCHS=2, temperature 1.0).

05/08 includes a fully worked numerical example (rewards {1.2,0.2,1.2,0.0} → advantages ±0.99/-0.81/-1.17, clip engagement traced for both positive and negative advantage) and the RLOO leave-one-out identity A_i = G/(G-1)(R_i - mean).

05/08 covers the 2025 fixes with exact loss algebra: Dr. GRPO token-level loss (sum/total-tokens), dropping std-normalization, DAPO clip-higher, dynamic sampling, overlong filtering, plus the dead-group (all-equal-reward, zero-gradient) failure mode as a first-class monitored metric.

05/09 builds a real math verifier: extract_boxed_answer with nested-brace walking and <answer> fallback, normalize_numeric handling \frac, fractions, percentages, Fraction-exact comparison with float atol fallback — followed by five assert-based sanity tests (verification with expected values).

05/09 builds a sandboxed code verifier with POSIX rlimits (CPU 5s, 512MB AS, NPROC 64), subprocess timeout, python -I isolated mode, hidden-test harness, graded fraction-of-tests reward, and explicitly flags it as illustrative vs container/microVM for production.

05/09 has a complete rlvr_reward with contingent format bonus and degeneracy guard, plus a worked G=6 GRPO group tracing mean 0.567, sigma 0.502, and all six advantages — including the case where the verifier saves a '1.0'-vs-'1' answer.

05/07 derives DPO end-to-end (closed-form optimum, implicit reward, BT cancellation of log Z), gives dpo_loss with the numerically-stable softplus(-Delta) form, a training step with frozen-reference no_grad passes, reward_acc/margin diagnostics, a hand-worked numerical update, beta guidance (0.1 default, 0.1-0.5 range), and the chosen-logprob-collapse failure mode with four fixes.

05/06 provides the full PPO stack (REINFORCE derivation, GAE recursion code, clipped surrogate, value clip, KL-folded-into-reward, entropy bonus, two-phase loop code, TARGET_KL early stopping) with healthy-clipfrac ranges (0.1-0.3) and the four-model memory accounting.

06-rl-infra/03 maps every algorithm to TRL (SFTTrainer, DPOTrainer, PPOTrainer, GRPOTrainer with a fully annotated runnable GSM8K-style math example, vLLM backend, monitoring health metrics); 06-rl-infra/08 gives production verifier/sandbox/reward-server architecture with throughput arithmetic.

Safety alignment (optional deliverable) is covered with code in 05/11: constitution-driven critique-revision, AI preference dataset construction, RLAIF labeling protocol, rejection sampling/STaR/ReST.
Appendix 05-from-scratch-index cross-links standalone DPO and GRPO from-scratch implementations, forming a deliberate SFT → DPO → GRPO build arc.
summary: Reference-grade coverage. Every deliverable of Assignment 5 is independently buildable from the book alone: a complete SFT loop with correct response-only loss masking and shift/padding edge cases (05/01, with multi-turn ChatML masking in 05/02); a real math verifier with answer extraction, Fraction-exact equivalence, and assert-based tests plus a sandboxed code verifier (05/09, production view in 06/08); a self-contained runnable GRPO trainer with worked numerical advantage/clip examples, RLOO, k3 KL, the Dr. GRPO/DAPO fixes, hyperparameters, and failure modes (05/08, PPO foundations in 05/06); and full DPO with derivation, stable implementation, worked update, and variants (05/07), plus safety alignment via CAI/RLAIF (05/11). Verification (asserts, worked numbers, diagnostic thresholds like clipfrac and reward_acc), resource accounting (memory budgets, throughput arithmetic), and library mappings (TRL GRPOTrainer/DPOTrainer, veRL, OpenRLHF) are all present. Deficits are small: an off-by-one/double-BOS mask-boundary bug in the flagship SFT example code, loss on trailing EOS-padding in the GRPO rollout, a population-vs-sample std inconsistency between worked example and code, and no expected end-to-end convergence signature for the toy GRPO run.
